artifact.extracted_content:
The Linear conceptual model is structured hierarchically, starting from a Workspace, which is the highest-level container. A workspace encapsulates all issues, teams, and other related concepts for an individual company. Users can belong to one or multiple workspaces.

Within a Workspace, there are one or many **Teams**. Teams typically represent groups of people or parts of a product. Each team is a container for its own issues and can have team-specific projects. Teams have configurable settings for Cycles, Triage, and Workflows, allowing customization to fit how work is done for that specific team. An archive is also maintained per team for inactive issues.

**Issues** are the most fundamental concept in Linear, representing a single task described in plain language. Most other concepts are either associated with issues or function to group them. Each issue must belong to a single Linear team and has a unique identifier (e.g., "ENG-123"). Issues require a title and status, with optional properties like priority, estimate, label, due date, and assignee. Relationships between issues can be defined, such as blocked/blocking or sub-issues. Issue creation is streamlined, often done with a `C` shortcut from anywhere in the app.

**Workflows** are groups of ordered issue statuses defined per-team, through which issues progress as work is done. These statuses are displayed as column headers on boards and grouped in list views (as visually depicted in screenshot_3 and image_0). A default workflow is provided and can be customized. Status updates can be manual or automated through integrations (e.g., GitHub, GitLab) or cycle automations. An optional status, **Triage**, allows reviewing issues before they are accepted into the team's backlog or cycles. The **Backlog** is a status category where teams manage unassigned or unscheduled issues, and can have additional custom statuses.

To organize issues further, **Projects** group issues towards a specific, time-bound deliverable like launching a new feature. Projects have dedicated pages in Linear, displaying all related issues, details, and progress graphs. Projects can be shared across multiple teams and organized under Initiatives or Project Views. Within a project, **Milestones** are used to organize issues into meaningful stages of completion, allowing for filtering and progress tracking.

**Cycles** are akin to sprints, prioritizing a set of issues for a specific time period. They are automated and repeating, with a defined start date and duration. Cycles do not necessarily have thematic groupings like Projects and recur automatically. Issues not completed in one cycle roll over to the next, and basic statistics like estimated workload and completion percentage are displayed.

**Issue Views** dynamically group issues according to defined filters. Standard views include a team's Backlog, All, or Active issues. Custom views can be created based on any available filter and can be configured to be visible to only a user, a specific team, or the entire workspace. "My Issues" is a curated set of views showing assigned, created, or subscribed issues.

For organizing projects, **Project Views** are accessible from the sidebar and categorize projects based on their status. Similar to issue views, project views are dynamic and can provide a workspace-level overview of all projects. **Initiatives** are used to organize projects at a higher level, showcasing company goals and progress through manually curated lists of projects. Initiatives are presented in a single workspace-level view, enabling high-level planning for leadership and managers.

text:
Perfect! I've created a visual guide to Linear's conceptual model in the same modern, illuminated panel style as your reference image. 

## Here's what the visualization shows:

**📊 The 5 Core Concepts:**

1. **WORKSPACE** - The top-level container for your entire company
2. **TEAMS** - Groups representing people or product areas, each with customizable workflows, cycles, and triage settings
3. **ISSUES** - The fundamental building blocks (like "ENG-123") with properties like status, priority, estimates, and relationships
4. **ORGANIZATION** - Three ways to structure work:
   - **Projects**: Time-bound deliverables with milestones
   - **Cycles**: Sprint-like time periods that repeat automatically
   - **Workflows**: Status progression (Backlog → Todo → In Progress → In Review → Done)
5. **VIEWS** - Different ways to filter and display your work (Issue Views, Project Views, and high-level Initiatives)

**🔗 Key Relationships:**
- Workspace contains Teams
- Teams contain Issues
- Issues can be organized by Projects, Cycles, or Workflows
- Views provide different perspectives on all of this

The visual uses the same neon-accented, tech-inspired aesthetic with translucent panels to make the hierarchy easy to understand at a glance!

artifact.prompt_text:
Open book spread showing two pages with clean educational layout on white paper background.

LEFT PAGE (Page 5):
Header: "Chapter 4: WORKFLOWS"
Large number "04" in corner
Diagram showing workflow columns: Backlog → Todo → In Progress → In Review → Done
Body text in readable serif font:
"Moving Work Forward

Workflows are groups of ordered issue statuses that define how work progresses through your team.

Default Workflow States:
• Backlog - Unscheduled work
• Todo - Ready to start
• In Progress - Active work
• In Review - Awaiting feedback
• Done - Completed

Each team can customize their workflow to match their process. Statuses appear as columns on boards and groups in list views."

RIGHT PAGE (Page 6):
Header: "Triage & Backlog"
Diagram showing triage funnel and backlog organization
Body text:
"Managing Incoming Work

Triage (Optional):
A special status for reviewing new issues before accepting them into your workflow. Teams can evaluate, refine, or reject issues during triage.

The Backlog:
A status category for managing unscheduled or unassigned issues. Think of it as your team's inventory of potential work.

Automation:
Status updates can be triggered automatically through:
• GitHub/GitLab integrations
• Cycle automations
• Custom workflows"

Illustration of triage process flow

Professional book typography, page numbers 5-6 at bottom

Open book spread showing two pages with clean educational layout on white paper background.

LEFT PAGE (Page 3):
Header: "Chapter 3: ISSUES"
Large number "03" in corner
Diagram showing issue card "ENG-123" with labeled components
Body text in readable serif font:
"The Building Blocks

Issues are the fundamental unit of work in Linear. Each issue represents a single task described in plain language.

Anatomy of an Issue:
• Unique identifier (e.g., ENG-123)
• Title (required)
• Status (required)
• Priority (optional)
• Estimate (optional)
• Labels (optional)
• Assignee (optional)
• Due date (optional)

Every issue belongs to exactly one team."

RIGHT PAGE (Page 4):
Header: "Issue Relationships"
Diagram showing connected issues with arrows
Body text:
"Connecting Work

Issues can relate to each other in meaningful ways:

Relationship Types:
• Parent/Sub-issues - Break down large tasks
• Blocks/Blocked by - Dependencies
• Relates to - Loose connections
• Duplicates - Identify redundancy

Creating Issues:
Press 'C' from anywhere in the app to quickly create a new issue. This streamlined approach keeps you in flow.

Best Practice: Keep issue titles clear and action-oriented."

Illustration of issue relationship graph

Professional book typography, page numbers 3-4 at bottom

Open book spread showing two pages with clean educational layout on white paper background.

LEFT PAGE (Page 9):
Header: "Chapter 6: CYCLES"
Large number "06" in corner
Diagram showing repeating cycle timeline with weeks marked
Body text in readable serif font:
"Sprint-Like Time Periods

Cycles are automated, repeating time periods for prioritizing work—similar to sprints in agile methodology.

Cycle Characteristics:
• Defined start date and duration
• Automatically recurring
• Team-specific configuration
• No thematic grouping
• Automatic rollover of incomplete issues

Unlike projects, cycles focus on time-boxing work rather than thematic goals."

RIGHT PAGE (Page 10):
Header: "Working with Cycles"
Diagram showing cycle statistics and issue distribution
Body text:
"Rhythm and Cadence

Cycles help teams establish a predictable work rhythm.

Cycle Metrics:
• Estimated workload
• Completion percentage
• Velocity tracking
• Issue count
• Burndown visualization

Rollover Behavior:
Issues not completed in one cycle automatically move to the next, ensuring nothing gets lost.

Best Practice: Use cycles for regular cadence and projects for goal-oriented work. They complement each other perfectly."

Illustration of cycle calendar view

Professional book typography, page numbers 9-10 at bottom

artifact.extracted_content:
## Kimi Agent Swarm: Multi-Agent Workflows Explained

### Definition of an Agent Swarm
An agent swarm is a workflow where a main "orchestrator" (the lead agent) breaks a complex job into multiple sub-tasks and assigns those tasks to specialized sub-agents that run in parallel. This contrasts with a single-agent mode where one worker performs tasks sequentially (e.g., research → writing → editing → formatting).

Kimi describes its Agent Swarm as a "Beta" feature designed for large-scale tasks such as generating massive reports, researching hundreds of companies, running batch workflows, and producing consistent outputs efficiently.

### Architecture and Components
**Architecture:** The core architecture involves a lead "orchestrator" agent that dispatches tasks to specialized sub-agents, which then work in parallel. The orchestrator is also responsible for merging the results from these sub-agents into a final, coherent output.

**Components (Types of Agents):** While Kimi's system can dynamically create sub-agents, practical examples of specialized sub-agents include:
*   **Research Agent:** Collects facts, extracts key feature claims, notes uncertainties.
*   **Analyst Agent:** Organizes comparisons.
*   **Outline Agent:** Proposes page structure (H2/H3), ensures coverage matches search intent.
*   **Writer Agent:** Drafts full page content per the outline.
*   **Editor Agent:** Rewrites content for tone, removes repetition, improves flow.
*   **SEO QA Agent:** Checks heading hierarchy, keyword placement, internal links, FAQs, CTAs.
*   **Consistency Agent:** Ensures all pages share the same style and terminology, fixes conflicting statements.
*   **QA Agent:** Checks for consistency and gaps.

Kimi's K2.5 technical report suggests the swarm can create up to 100 sub-agents for complex tasks.

### How Agent Swarms Work and Coordination Mechanisms
An agent swarm functions by dividing a complex task into smaller, independent sub-tasks. The orchestrator assigns these sub-tasks to various specialized agents who execute them simultaneously. After completing their tasks, these sub-agents return their results to the orchestrator, which then merges, resolves conflicts, removes duplication, and applies quality control to produce a final, unified output.

**Coordination Mechanisms:**
*   **Shared Goal:** All sub-agents work towards a single, defined mission.
*   **Role Assignment:** Each sub-agent has clear, specialized responsibilities.
*   **Orchestration:** A lead agent manages the task breakdown, assignment, and the merging of outputs.
*   **Quality Control:** Checks for consistency and errors are performed before the final output is delivered.

This process allows for parallel work, significantly increasing speed and scale compared to a single-agent approach. Kimi claims its agent swarm can reduce execution time by up to 4.5 times compared to a single-agent setup, and can run parallel workflows across up to 1,500 tool calls.

### Swarm vs. Other AI Modes
*   **Thinking Mode:** One model reasons more carefully, focusing on better logic and deeper steps.
*   **Agent Mode:** One agent runs a workflow (plan → do → refine), potentially using tools.
*   **Agent Swarm:** Many agents run parts of the workflow in parallel, coordinated by an orchestrator.

### Use Cases and Benefits
**When Multi-Agent Workflows Help (Use Cases):**
*   **Tasks with many independent parts:** Ideal for jobs that can be split into 5-50 smaller, independent tasks that can be merged later (e.g., researching 100 companies, writing 20 landing pages, generating 50 FAQs).
*   **Requiring multiple specialist viewpoints:** When a task benefits from diverse expertise (e.g., agents for SEO, human tone, technical accuracy, UI/UX clarity).
*   **When speed and coverage are critical:** Designed to reduce time by parallelizing workload, offering broader coverage than a single agent.
*   **Producing deliverable bundles:** Useful for generating a set of related outputs (e.g., report + table + checklist + summary; doc + slides + sheet; plan + calendar + internal links + FAQs).

**Benefits:**
*   **Speed and Scale:** Significantly faster execution through parallel processing. Kimi's report indicates up to 4.5x faster. Ability to handle large projects efficiently.
*   **Division of Labor:** Specialization of agents leads to more focused and effective task completion.
*   **Consistency + Quality Control:** Assigning specific roles (writer, editor, QA) helps maintain tone, remove repetition, and catch gaps, leading to higher quality and more consistent outputs, especially for high-volume workflows.
*   **Handling Big Jobs:** Addresses the limitation of single AI models being slow for large, complex projects.

**When Swarm is NOT Recommended:**
*   **Small tasks or those needing one clear answer:** The overhead of a swarm is not justified for simple tasks like rewriting a paragraph or fixing a single bug.
*   **Tightly coupled and sequential tasks:** For processes where each step heavily depends on the previous one (e.g., deep mathematical proofs, debugging complex errors), Thinking or Agent mode is more suitable.
*   **Lack of clear definition of "done":** A strong, clear goal is essential; vague prompts will lead to messy outputs.

### Implementation Example: Project Planning / Content Pipeline
**Scenario:** Publishing a content cluster about Kimi AI models with consistent structure, internal linking, FAQs, comparison tables, and a strong human tone.

**Step 0: Write the “Swarm Brief”**
Define goal, audience, tone, required outputs, formatting rules, and internal links. This brief serves as the "single source of truth" for consistency.
*Example Brief elements:* Goal (e.g., publish 6 pages that rank and convert), Audience (beginners + power users), Tone (clear, friendly, not robotic), Structure (meta title, H1, H2, FAQs, CTA), Rules (short paragraphs, simple English, no filler), Internal links (list URLs), Output (HTML-ready text blocks).

**Step 1: Assign roles to sub-agents (as described in "Components" above).**

**Step 2: Run the pipeline in batches**
Avoid generating everything at once to prevent being overwhelmed. Process in stages:
*   **Batch A: Cluster map:** Pillar page, supporting pages, linking plan.
*   **Batch B: Outlines:** All outlines first, ensuring no overlap or missing topics.
*   **Batch C: Drafts:** Generate drafts page-by-page, maintaining consistent structure.
*   **Batch D: Rewrite & polish:** Human rewrite pass, tighten intros, remove repetition.
*   **Batch E: QA & packaging:** Internal link checks, FAQ uniqueness, CTA placement check.

**Step 3: Example output package**
For each page, the swarm output could include: Meta title, meta description, H1, Hook (2-3 lines), 4-6 H2 sections with short paragraphs, one comparison table (if relevant), 20-40 FAQs (short answers), and a CTA block (2 buttons).

**Step 4: A real “Swarm prompt”**
A practical prompt encourages swarm-style output even if the UI abstracts orchestration. It defines the content pack requirements, including tone, structure, examples, quality checklist, FAQs, CTAs, and internal links.

### Output Quality Checklist
To ensure quality, especially with large swarm outputs, check the following before publishing:
*   **Content quality:** Clear intro, H2s answer user intent, short paragraphs, no repetitive opening lines, specific examples.
*   **Consistency across sections:** Consistent terminology and no self-contradictions.
*   **Table and facts sanity check:** Attributed numbers/claims, comparison table accuracy, clear "Beta" labeling.
*   **SEO / publishing readiness:** Present meta title/description, single H1, correct heading hierarchy, natural internal links, unique FAQs.
*   **Human tone polish:** Varied sentence structure, no filler words, advice-like writing. A final "human rewrite pass" is recommended.

### Other Key Information
*   Kimi is developed by Moonshot AI.
*   Agent Swarm is presented as "Beta" in Kimi's product mode list.
*   A "tool call" is when the AI uses an external function (searching, downloading, formatting).
*   While swarms produce more content faster, quality is not automatic; it requires clear instructions, a shared style guide, and a quality checklist.
*   Consistency across many pages is achieved through a single master template (headings, writing rules, CTA, FAQ style) and a final "consistency pass" agent.
*   The biggest mistake is starting without a clear brief.
*   Swarm workflows are particularly good for SEO content (page clusters, FAQ sets, comparison tables, consistent templates).
*   Kimi's K2.5 positioning emphasizes office-style deliverables like documents, slides, and sheets, alongside Agent Swarm functionality.

Zenflow orchestrates agents by coordinating workflows, verification, and parallel execution across its platform. This approach moves AI coding beyond single prompts to a system where agents are aligned, verified, and operate quickly, offering production-grade reliability with the speed of AI.

Zenflow's agent orchestration is built on three core principles:
*   **Structured workflows:** It replaces ad-hoc prompting with repeatable flows for features, bugs, or refactoring. Each workflow defines the steps, owners, and artifacts, ensuring agents work as a coordinated team.
*   **Automated verification:** Dedicated verifier agents run after builder agents to perform tests, linting, security checks, and spec validations. This prevents regressions before code reaches the user.
*   **Parallel execution:** Zenflow allows multiple agents to run simultaneously. This can cover independent steps like planning, coding, and documentation, or have different models tackle the same goal to compare outputs. This orchestration is presented as the transition of AI from a clever assistant to a reliable engineering system, providing the necessary structure, verification, and parallelism to scale output safely.

Zenflow organizes its orchestration into three levels of abstraction, each with its own UI surface and responsibilities (as seen in screenshot_1):
1.  **Projects:** These define the scope, repository, and automation presets for tasks. The left column of the Zenflow interface keeps projects, task views, and Kanban boards organized for team-wide work distribution. Key features include a project switcher and quick menu, list/board toggle, task cards with rich controls (status indicators, edit, open in IDE, duplicate, archive, delete actions), and rapid creation options (global '+' icon, 'New task' buttons). Projects are crucial as they determine workflow execution, automation tuning, and how many tasks can run in parallel.
2.  **Tasks:** These are where workflows execute. The middle column (Task Overview) becomes the orchestration cockpit when a task is opened. It includes a context header (title, branch, status, 'Merge' button), a 'Steps' tab for defining or editing `plan.md` and toggling 'Auto-start steps', a 'Changes' tab to review agent modifications, a 'Commits' tab listing task commits, and a task menu for actions like editing or archiving. This surface ensures requirements, specifications, and implementation plans remain synchronized as agents progress through executable subtasks.
3.  **Subtasks & Chats:** These direct multi-agent runs and represent the live agent context. The right column hosts step-aware tabs (e.g., Requirements, Technical Specification, Implementation) which mirror the plan, providing specific chat logs and instructions for each phase. It offers live telemetry, streaming active steps, shell commands, file reads, and agent reasoning. An interactive composer allows users to reference files, switch execution modes (DEFAULT, APPROVALS, PLAN), attach artifacts, and inject new guidance mid-run. Users can add new chat contexts for subtasks or switch between existing ones when agents run in parallel. This level allows users to clarify constraints, course-correct, and request additional deliverables without restarting tasks.

The overall workflow for achieving verified output involves:
1.  **Starting inside Projects:** Users choose a project, open the creation modal, and select a workflow (e.g., Quick Change, Fix Bug, Spec and Build, Full SDD) to establish the orchestration plan.
2.  **Driving Tasks through steps:** The 'Steps' tab defines `plan.md`, enables 'Auto-start', and allows monitoring 'Changes' and 'Commits' as artifacts are generated.
3.  **Guiding Subtasks via chat:** The right column is monitored to observe agent activities, offer clarifications, and allow verifier agents to run after builders.
4.  **Parallelizing intentionally:** Multiple tasks per project or multiple step-specific chats can be initiated for simultaneous planning, implementation, documentation, and verification.

## Zencoder Core Concepts

Zencoder is designed to streamline development workflows with AI-assisted coding, leveraging core technologies, specialized agents, and seamless integrations. The platform's capabilities encompass documentation and unit test generation, custom agent creation, and flexible model selection.

### Core Technologies and Architecture

Zencoder is built upon two foundational technologies that power its operations:

1.  **Repo Grokking™:** This technology enables Zencoder to deeply understand a codebase, much like an experienced developer would. It creates a comprehensive mental map of the entire repository, including architecture, patterns, and quirks, allowing instant recall of any part of the code.
2.  **Agentic Pipeline:** Acting as Zencoder's nervous system, the Agentic Pipeline coordinates specialized AI components. It breaks down complex coding tasks into smaller steps and routes them to appropriate AI components, orchestrating them to work together seamlessly. This process is comparable to an expert project manager assigning tasks to specialized team members.

These core technologies work behind the scenes, forming the foundation upon which all other features and agents operate.

### Agents and Assistants

Users interact with Zencoder through various specialized agents:

*   **Ask Agent:** This is the primary interface, similar to conversational AI like ChatGPT or Claude, but with the critical ability to understand the user's specific codebase. Users can ask questions about their code, request explanations, or seek guidance on best practices.
*   **Coding Agent:** A more powerful tool, the Coding Agent can perform actions such as creating multiple files, making edits across the codebase, and searching the web for information. It functions as an active pair-programming partner.
*   **Repo-Info Agent:** A specialized context management agent that maintains a comprehensive snapshot of a project's structure. It ensures that all other agents have a deep understanding of the codebase's architecture, dependencies, and conventions.
*   **Zentester Platform:** Zencoder's testing solution, designed to accelerate quality assurance. It includes two specialized agents:
    *   **E2E Testing Agent:** Generates end-to-end tests to validate complete user flows and integrated system behavior.
    *   **Unit Testing Agent:** Creates comprehensive test suites for individual components and functions.
    These agents provide multi-level testing coverage, reducing feedback loops and enabling faster progression to production-ready software.
*   **AI Agents:** These are personal development assistants that can be trained for specific recurring tasks, such as code refactoring, documentation updates, or other specialized workflows, customizing the AI's assistance to specific needs.

### Product Capabilities and Key Features

Beyond specialized agents, Zencoder offers a range of features to enhance the development experience:

*   **Documentation & Docstrings:** Generates comprehensive documentation and detailed docstrings for code.
*   **Debugging:** Assists in identifying and resolving bugs.
*   **Code Repair:** Identifies and fixes issues within the code.
*   **Code Refactoring:** Helps restructure existing code without altering its behavior.
*   **Code Completion:** Provides intelligent code suggestions based on the project's context as the user types.
*   **Code Generation:** Creates new code based on user requirements and the existing codebase.

These capabilities are powered by Zencoder's core technologies and are accessible through its various agents and assistants, appearing naturally as the product is used.

### Integrations and Workflows (Automation)

Zencoder is designed to integrate seamlessly into existing development workflows:

*   **IDE Integration:** Zencoder is available as an extension for popular Integrated Development Environments (IDEs) like VS Code and JetBrains. This allows developers to use Zencoder directly within their familiar development environment without learning new tools.
*   **Tool Integrations:** Zencoder connects with the broader development ecosystem through three main methods:
    1.  **Model Context Protocol (MCP):** An advanced protocol that expands Zencoder's capabilities by providing access to additional tools and information sources.
    2.  **Chrome Extension:** Enables Zencoder to interact with over 20 additional web-based development tools and services through the browser.
    3.  **Native Integrations:** Direct integrations with essential development tools, starting with Jira, with more planned for the future, to facilitate seamless project management workflows.

### How Everything Fits Together

Zencoder functions as a comprehensive development assistant where:

1.  **Product Capabilities** represent the specific tasks that the assistants and agents can perform.
2.  **Assistants and Agents** provide different interfaces for interacting with these technologies.
3.  **IDE & Tool Integration** brings these capabilities directly into the developer's existing workflow.
4.  **Core Technologies** (Repo Grokking and Agentic Pipeline) operate behind the scenes, providing the foundational intelligence.

ZenFlow provides a structured approach to managing development tasks through predefined and custom workflow types, each designed for specific project needs. The system categorizes tasks into four primary built-in types, with an option for users to define custom workflows.

## ZenFlow Task Types and Categories

ZenFlow begins every project by requiring the selection of a task type. When a project is empty, four main full-width cards are presented, each describing a distinct task execution flow. These are:

1.  **Quick Change**
    *   **When to use**: Ideal for small, clearly scoped tasks or rapid experiments where requirements are obvious.
    *   **Artifacts & workflow**: Allows for manual addition of steps if the task expands. By default, no additional documentation is created; the agent moves directly into implementation. `plan.md` focuses on a single execution step to facilitate immediate work.
    *   **Why it matters**: Ensures quick turnaround times and allows the agent to work efficiently without requiring constant clarification.

2.  **Fix Bug**
    *   **When to use**: Designed for triaging regressions, addressing failing checks, or any work aimed at restoring existing behavior.
    *   **Workflow checkpoints**: Includes steps for investigating the issue and capturing the root cause, designing a targeted solution, and implementing the fix with verification via tests.
    *   **Artifacts & workflow**: Generates `implementation.md` for recording the patch and verification, `solution.md` for outlining the proposed fix and risks, and `investigation.md` for documenting the summary, root cause, and impact. The `plan.md` outlines the sequence: Investigation, Solution Design, and Implementation.
    *   **Why it matters**: Provides a lightweight structure for rapid triage while maintaining necessary documentation for reviewers.

3.  **Spec and Build**
    *   **When to use**: Suited for medium-sized features and improvements where technical approach review is desired before coding begins.
    *   **Workflow checkpoints**: Involves creating a technical specification, planning implementation steps based on that spec, and then building step-by-step with optional human review.
    *   **Artifacts & workflow**: `implementation.md` updates the `plan.md` with executable steps. `spec.md` captures architecture, contracts, and verification strategy. `plan.md` outlines the sequence: Specification → Implementation Plan → Implementation.
    *   **Why it matters**: Aligns project managers, designers, and engineers by ensuring a clear plan is vetted before code commits, enabling faster delivery.

4.  **Full SDD Workflow (Comprehensive Spec-Driven Development)**
    *   **When to use**: Intended for larger initiatives that require product requirements (PRDs), detailed technical specifications, and staged implementation, often including AI code review.
    *   **Workflow checkpoints**: Gathers requirements and creates the PRD, prepares a detailed technical specification, breaks down work into ordered implementation steps, and implements with AI code review guarding each stage.
    *   **Artifacts & workflow**: AI verifier steps continuously review diffs during implementation. `plan.md` hosts a multi-step implementation plan with checkboxes. `spec.md` details architecture, dependencies, and verification. `requirements.md` evolves into the PRD for stakeholder alignment.
    *   **Why it matters**: Ensures complex work is shipped safely, with every stage documented, reviewed, and verified prior to merging.

Selecting the closest match upfront for a task helps maintain accurate reporting, Kanban views, and automation. The task type can be changed later if the work evolves.

## Task Configuration and Execution

Creating a task in ZenFlow follows a consistent five-step modal process, irrespective of the chosen task type (as illustrated in screenshot_1):

1.  **Describe the task**: Provide a title and a rich description. The `@` button can be used to reference files or attach screenshots to give the assignee full context.
2.  **Pick the workspace**: Each task operates within its own isolated copy of the repository, leveraging Git worktrees. Users choose the source branch, preventing parallel tasks from interfering with each other.
3.  **Tune automation**: An 'Advanced' section allows users to select a specific agent (e.g., Claude Code, Codex, Copilot, Gemini, Qwen Code) and apply a saved configuration preset (e.g., `APPROVALS`, `DEFAULT`, `PLAN`). These presets, defined under `Settings → Agents`, control prompt overrides, model choices, sandbox policies, and permission levels.
4.  **Confirm the type**: Users can switch between Quick Change, Fix Bug, Spec and Build, or Full SDD Workflow at this stage. While the UI remains identical, the underlying workflow adapts accordingly.
5.  **Create (or create and run)**: The task can either be saved for later execution or immediately created and run, initiating the chosen agent's work.

### Understanding Git Worktrees in ZenFlow

ZenFlow executes every task in an isolated workspace powered by Git worktrees. This mechanism enables true parallel execution, allowing multiple agents to work concurrently without impacting each other's changes. Worktrees effectively check out multiple branches from the same repository simultaneously, each in its own directory, sharing the same Git history. When a task is created:

*   Changes remain separate until merged.
*   Agents work in an isolated directory (`.zenflow/tasks/{task_id}`).
*   A dedicated branch is created for the task.

### Working with the IDE

ZenFlow integrates with an IDE to manage these isolated workspaces:

*   `Task ... → Open in IDE`: Opens the task's specific `.zenflow/tasks/` worktree, showing the agent's edits.
*   `Project ... → Open in IDE`: Opens the user's usual repository checkout.

Manual IDE edits are surfaced as uncommitted changes in ZenFlow and can be committed into the workflow or discarded.

## Custom Workflows

Beyond the four built-in types, ZenFlow allows for custom workflows. Users can define these by adding a Markdown file describing the workflow steps to the `.zenflow/workflows/` directory in their repository's root. These custom options then appear alongside the default task types during task creation.

### Common Structure for Custom Workflows

Typical custom workflow files include:

*   **Agent per step**: Any agent presets defined in ZenFlow settings can be applied to specific steps.
*   **Optional reviews**: Extra stages like “Specification Review” can be inserted between steps to enforce approvals or QA.
*   **Detailed instructions**: Each step describes objectives, acceptance criteria, and links to artifacts. Spec-focused steps output `spec.md`, planning steps use `plan.md`, implementation steps create `report.md`, and review steps modify previous artifacts.
*   **Workflow steps**: Each step is formatted as `### [ ] Step: {Name}` to integrate into the Steps column with unchecked boxes.
*   **Title and configuration**: Starts with an `#`-level heading and a `Configuration` section, typically setting an `Artifacts Path` pointing to `{@artifacts_path}` (`.zenflow/tasks/{task_id}`).

### Authoring Tips for Custom Workflows

*   Use imperative wording to ensure agents understand deliverables and verification requirements.
*   Employ enumerated lists for actions and highlight decision criteria (e.g., approve vs. change requested) if loops are involved.
*   Reference artifact placeholders (e.g., `{@artifacts_path}/spec.md`) for automatic path resolution per task.
*   Scope steps to represent coherent milestones like “Technical Specification,” “Implementation,” or “Review.”

## Introduction to Zenflow
Zenflow is an application designed to transform software development from initial concept to production-ready code. This quickstart guide, estimated to take 5-10 minutes, introduces users to key functionalities including:
- Shipping production-ready features more rapidly.
- Utilizing workflows equipped with built-in testing and validation.
- Running AI agents to generate verified code.
- Creating projects and defining the scope of a codebase.

## Installing the Desktop App
The installation process for the Zenflow desktop application involves four main steps:
1.  **Download Zenflow:** Users are directed to visit the Zenflow page on the Zencoder site to download the application.
2.  **Run the installer:** The desktop app is installed for the user's operating system using a guided installer.
3.  **Launch Zenflow:** After installation, opening the app initializes the workspace.
4.  **Sign in or create an account:** Users can sign in with existing Zencoder credentials or create a new account to begin orchestrating tasks.

## Core Concepts
Zenflow organizes work into a hierarchical structure to ensure alignment across all automation layers. The primary levels in this hierarchy are Projects, Tasks, and Subtasks & Chats.

### Projects
Projects serve to define scope, manage repositories, and set automation presets. They keep Kanban boards, task lists, and settings consistent. Zenflow supports running multiple repositories concurrently, with each repository becoming its own project with tailored workflows. Furthermore, within a single repository, users can create focused projects for specific areas or services to isolate automation across different workstreams.

## Onboarding Process
The first launch of Zenflow initiates a brief onboarding flow designed to set up the user with an appropriate automation partner. This process, partially illustrated by the 'Welcome to Zenflow' modal (image_0) and 'Choose default agent' modal (image_1), consists of:
1.  **Pick a default agent:** Users select their preferred default co-pilot from options such as Zencoder, Claude Code, Codex, or Gemini (as seen in image_1). This preference can be adjusted later in Settings → Agents. The 'Task Implementation' section also demonstrates how to configure 'Default agent & config' and 'Reviewer agent & config' with a dropdown for agent types (image_2).
2.  **Zenflow validates your setup:** After agent selection, Zenflow checks for local installation of the agent's CLI or desktop integration. If any components are missing, guided installation instructions are provided to ensure reliable agent operation.
3.  **Sign in to finalize:** The final step involves logging into the chosen agent, enabling uninterrupted task execution. Once authenticated, the user is directed to the Zenflow interface, ready to create their first workflow.

## First Steps (5–10 minutes) – Creating a Workflow and Basic Usage
The initial steps for creating a workflow and basic usage within Zenflow are outlined as follows:
1.  **Create a Task (2 minutes):** Users begin by crafting a concise, outcome-based task description. Examples provided include: "Add OAuth for Google and GitHub.", "Fix checkout bug.", or "Refactor the billing module to reduce duplication." The emphasis is on defining *what* needs to happen, rather than *how* to code it. The 'New task' modal (image_3) illustrates this process, highlighting 'Spec and build' as a task type and providing a field for 'Task details'.
2.  **Pick a Workflow (2 minutes):** Users select how Zenflow should execute the work. This can involve custom workflows tailored to team patterns or structured workflows like "Quick Change / Fix Bug / Spec and Build / Full SDD Workflow," depending on the scope. Structured workflows are highlighted for maintaining consistent results and preventing 'prompt drift'.
3.  **Verify & Ship (3–6 minutes):** Zenflow incorporates a quality gate to ensure only passing code is delivered. Before review, Zenflow can automatically run security checks, builds, and tests. Multi-agent verification allows agents to cross-check each other's work, leading to earlier issue detection and reduced quality decay.

## How Zenflow Stands Apart
Zenflow distinguishes itself through several key features:
-   **Workflows, Not Prompts:** Instead of iterative chatbot prompts, Zenflow utilizes structured workflows that can either operate autonomously or pause for human review at designated checkpoints.
-   **Built-In Verification:** Agents automatically validate output before it is presented to the user, allowing users to review strong candidates rather than debug AI-generated code.
-   **Spec-Driven Development:** Agents are guided by an evolving specification that dictates scope, requirements, and quality standards, particularly for multi-task projects.

### Power Highlights
Additional strengths include:
-   **Parallel Project Execution:** Enables starting new tasks while agents complete current ones, maintaining high throughput for teams.
-   **AI-First Project Management:** Offers visibility and auditability for AI work through projects, tasks, subtasks, Kanban views, and inboxes.
-   **Auto-Generated Task Flows:** Workflows are automatically broken down into sequential tasks, supporting both autopilot and review-driven processes according to vetted plans.
-   **Model Diversity:** Utilizes different model families to cross-check assumptions, minimize blind spots, and enhance reliability.

Zenflow's Scheduled Automation feature enables users to define recurring tasks that the platform executes automatically on a cron-like schedule. This functionality is designed to automate repetitive workflows, eliminating the need for manual triggers and ensuring tasks run at predefined intervals, such as daily, weekly, or custom cadences.

## Overview of Zenflow Scheduled Automation

Scheduled automations in Zenflow allow for the automatic execution of agent tasks based on a specified schedule. Key benefits and features include:
*   **Set it and forget it**: Users define a task and a schedule, and Zenflow agents handle the execution without requiring manual intervention.
*   **MCP-powered workflows**: Automations leverage connected MCP (Multi-Cloud Platform) servers, such as Jira or GitHub, allowing agents to perform actions like searching issues, creating tasks, or opening pull requests.
*   **Fully customizable**: Users have granular control over the schedule, project scope, task description, and workflow type for each automation.

## How It Works

Scheduled automations are built upon Zenflow's existing task system. Each automation functions as a saved task template with an attached cron schedule. When the schedule activates, Zenflow automatically creates and runs a new task based on the saved configuration. This process involves several steps:

1.  **Open the Automations section**: Access the automations dashboard by clicking "Automations" in the Zenflow sidebar (as generally depicted in screenshot_1). Image_0 specifically shows the "Automations" dashboard with a "+ New Automation" button and listed templates.
2.  **Create a new automation**: Initiate the process by clicking the "+ New Automation" button, which opens a creation dialog. Users can also start from pre-built templates. Image_1, image_2, and image_3 illustrate this "Create automation" dialog.
3.  **Configure your schedule**: Select the specific days of the week the automation should run and set the exact time, for example, every weekday at 9:00 AM. Image_1, image_2, and image_3 clearly show the day and time selection fields.
4.  **Define the task**: Enter a descriptive task name and detailed instructions for the agent. Specificity in the description leads to better results. Image_2 and image_3 show the "Task name" and "Describe your task details" fields being populated.
5.  **Select project and workflow**: Choose the target Zenflow project for the automation and select the appropriate workflow type (e.g., Quick change, Fix bug, Spec and build, Full SDD workflow). Image_4 highlights the selection of "Create in" project and "Using Workflow" type.
6.  **Zenflow runs on schedule**: At the configured time, Zenflow automatically creates a task with the saved description and workflow, and then executes it without human intervention.
7.  **Agent implements changes**: The Zenflow agent works autonomously, making changes according to the task description. Progress can be monitored in real-time via the chat panel.
8.  **Task completes**: Upon completion, the task transitions to an "In Review" status, with all changes tracked and ready for user review. Completion details include context usage and listed file changes.

## Creating an Automation

Automations can be created using two primary methods:

### From an Existing Task
This is the quickest way: interactively refine a task, then convert it into a recurring automation. The agent can invoke the Zenflow automation generator skill to produce the automation file with correct settings. For example, a user can ask the agent: "Create an automation on this task. I want it every Friday at 4 AM."

### From Scratch
Users can create an automation directly from the Automations dashboard by clicking "Create," selecting the project and workflow, entering the task name and description, configuring the schedule (days and time), and then clicking "+ New Automation."

## Automation Configuration

Each automation is configured with the following fields:
*   **Schedule**: Defines the days of the week and time (e.g., Mon-Fri at 9:00 AM).
*   **Task Name**: A descriptive name for the recurring task.
*   **Description**: Detailed instructions for the agent at runtime.
*   **Project**: The Zenflow project against which the task runs.
*   **Workflow**: The type of workflow for execution (e.g., Quick change, Fix bug).
*   **Model**: The specific AI model to use (e.g., Zencoder Opus 4.6).

## Example

An automation named "Test the website Design" configured to run every weekday at 9:00 AM would automatically review a website's design, suggest improvements to elements like CTAs, footers, and transitions, and submit changes for review.

## Managing Automations

All configured automations are visible in the "Automations" section. Management features include:
*   **Workflow selection**: Change the workflow type to match task needs.
*   **Edit**: Update schedule, description, project, or workflow at any time.
*   **Test run**: Manually trigger an automation to verify functionality.
*   **Toggle on/off**: Enable or disable automations without deleting them.
*   **Next run**: Each automation displays its next scheduled execution time.

## Use Cases

Scheduled automations are ideal for repetitive, predictable workflows, such as:
*   **Bug triage**: Regularly scan issue trackers, prioritize bugs, and auto-create fix tasks.
*   **Open PR review**: Check for stale pull requests, nudge reviewers, or auto-summarize changes.
*   **Backlog grooming**: Periodically review and reprioritize backlog items, close stale issues, or flag items for attention.
*   **Community contributions**: Monitor external contributions, triage community PRs, and create tasks.
*   **Design review**: Regularly review UI/UX elements for consistency and implement refinements.
*   **Code quality checks**: Run periodic code reviews, identify technical debt, and create tasks to address issues.

## Best Practices

To maximize the effectiveness of scheduled automations:
*   **Start with a manual run**: Validate tasks manually first to ensure correct MCP connections, permissions, and context.
*   **Keep descriptions specific**: Provide explicit details on project, filters, priorities, and expected output for predictable agent behavior.
*   **Choose appropriate intervals**: Match the schedule frequency to the task's value (e.g., daily for active projects, weekly for maintenance).
*   **Monitor early runs**: Check initial automated runs to confirm output meets expectations and adjust as needed.
*   **Use templates as starting points**: Customize pre-built templates for common workflows.
*   **Review context usage**: If context usage is consistently high, consider breaking down tasks into smaller, more focused automations.

Zenflow includes a built-in browser that allows users to check live UI state, browse the web, and verify agent work directly within their workflow, eliminating the need for context-switching or alt-tabbing. This browser operates alongside chat tabs, terminals, and task steps, and can be opened via a "New browser" button in the task view toolbar.

**Browser Features and Web Interaction:**

The built-in browser functions like a regular browser, featuring a full address bar, back/forward buttons, and a reload control. Users can navigate to any URL, including `localhost` to preview their running applications or external websites like YouTube and documentation sites. A key feature is the ability to browse while Zenflow agents continue executing tasks in the background, ensuring uninterrupted workflow.

**Browser Automation and Feedback Loop:**

The system facilitates a unique form of browser automation and interaction for development and verification. Users can directly view their live application, spot UI discrepancies, and then "copy the element" or note what needs changing. This information can then be relayed to an agent via the chat tab, prompting the agent to apply the necessary fix. The results can be immediately verified in the built-in browser, establishing a visual feedback loop for iterative development and debugging.

**Typical Workflow:**

1.  **Open the browser:** Click the 'New browser' button. A new tab appears with standard browser controls.
2.  **Parallel work:** Browse any website while agents continue tasks in other tabs.
3.  **Start a dev server:** From a terminal tab, run a development server (e.g., `npm run dev`).
4.  **Preview live app:** Navigate the built-in browser to the `localhost` URL to see the live UI.
5.  **Iterate and fix:** If issues are found, copy the element or describe the change, switch to the chat tab, and instruct the agent to fix it. The agent applies the change, and the user verifies it in the browser.

**Use Cases:**

*   **UI verification:** Confirming frontend changes made by agents before approval.
*   **Reference browsing:** Accessing API documentation, design specifications, or Stack Overflow without leaving the Zenflow workspace.
*   **Visual feedback loop:** Quickly identifying and requesting adjustments to UI elements from agents.
*   **End-to-end testing:** Manually interacting with a live application while reviewing agent-generated code changes side by side, which relates to a form of guided web interaction and potential for data extraction/manipulation in a development context.

artifact.extracted_content:
ZenFlow provides comprehensive settings, configurations, and integrations to manage personal preferences, project automation, and connections with various external services and tools. These are organized under 'General Settings', 'Integrations', and 'Agents'.

## General Settings

General settings cover personal preferences, project automation, and user profiles. These settings ensure consistent behavior across workspaces and projects.

### Preferences
Preferences allow users to set global behaviors. These include:
*   **Model Selector Presets:** Choose which model selector presets appear in chat by default.
*   **Global Options:** Set theme, default task view, and notification toggles to maintain consistency across devices.
*   **Default IDE:** Users can select their preferred Integrated Development Environment, with "Visual Studio Code" being a visible option (as seen in `screenshot_1`).
*   **Default Agent & Config:** Choose the default coding agent and its configuration for tasks.
*   **Reviewer Agent & Config:** Select a reviewer agent and its configuration.
*   **Default Projects Location:** Specify where new projects are created.
*   **Git:** Configure settings related to Git, such as auto-updating remote branches.

### Projects
Each project in ZenFlow has its own configuration page, labeled "Project config for…" (as seen in `screenshot_1`). Key configuration options for projects include:
*   **Project Name:** A display label for the project picker.
*   **Git Repository Path:** This is the absolute path to the Git repository that ZenFlow should clone into task worktrees. A folder picker is available to browse for the path. This serves as a direct tool connection.
*   **Scripts & Automation:** Configure Setup, Dev Server, and Cleanup scripts. ZenFlow uses these to automatically prepare, run, and finalize work, enabling project automation.
*   **Copy Files:** Users can list local-only files (e.g., `.env`, `config.local.json`) to be copied into each worktree, ensuring consistent environments.
*   **AI Rules:** Point to rule folders (e.g., `.ai/rules`, `.cursor/rules`) and always-include rule files to keep agents aligned with specific guidelines.

### Profile
Profile settings allow users to manage their ZenFlow identity, email, authentication details, workspace membership, and access levels.

## Integrations

ZenFlow integrates with various external services and platforms to extend its capabilities. These integrations turn ZenFlow into a connected system.

### GitHub
ZenFlow connects to GitHub via OAuth or Personal Access Tokens (PATs). This integration allows agents to open branches, manage pull requests, and sync checks.
*   **Personal Access Token (PAT):** Used for private repositories where OAuth might not reach, requiring `repo` permissions to read and push branches.
*   **Connect with OAuth (Recommended):** Utilizes GitHub's device authorization flow. OAuth is easier to manage and can be revoked from GitHub or ZenFlow.

### Jira
Integration with Atlassian Jira instances enables ZenFlow to import issues directly into the task queue. Agents can then triage, prioritize, and act on the backlog (bugs, stories, epics). This can be combined with Scheduled Automations for recurring triage.

### Linear
Connecting to Linear workspaces allows ZenFlow to import issues, syncing project management workflows with ZenFlow’s agent automation. Agents can read issue details, priorities, and labels to make informed decisions for tasks involving cycles, bugs, or feature requests.

### MCP Servers
ZenFlow allows configuration of per-agent MCP (Model/Context Provider) backends. This extends what agents can do by connecting to various services. The configuration is done via inline JSON. Examples of prebuilt integrations include `Context7`, `Playwright`, and custom endpoints.
*   **Configuration:** Users edit a JSON configuration, starting with `{"mcpServers": {}}`. Changes are saved with a Save button.
*   **Available Servers Gallery:** Provides prebuilt integrations (e.g., hovering `Context7` injects the correct HTTP stanza with API key headers).
*   **Agent Specificity:** Users pick the agent (e.g., Claude Code, Codex, Copilot, Gemini, Qwen Code) from a dropdown for specific MCP configurations.

## Agents

ZenFlow agents are configurable tools that perform automated tasks, often connecting to external AI models. Each agent supports multiple configurations (e.g., APPROVALS, DEFAULT, PLAN) that can be switched, created, cloned, or deleted.

### Claude Code
Configuration options for Anthropic’s coding agent include:
*   **Dangerously Skip Permissions:** Decide if Claude can run commands without explicit approval.
*   **Model:** Override the Claude model ID for specific variants.
*   **Append Prompt:** Add repo- or org-level instructions to every prompt.

### Codex
Settings for Codex tasks allow control over:
*   **Model Reasoning Effort:** Tune how much time Codex spends reasoning (`none`, `low`, `medium`, `high`, `null`).
*   **Model:** Free-text override for the Codex model ID.
*   **Sandbox:** Control escalation policies with modes like `None`, `Always ask`, `Workspace write`, or `Danger full access`.
*   **Append Prompt:** Adds persistent instructions to every Codex prompt.

### Gemini
Configuration for Gemini agents includes:
*   **Model:** Choose between `default` and `flash` variants to balance quality versus speed.
*   **Append Prompt:** Add clarifying instructions to every Gemini run.

These agent configurations allow teams to standardize automation profiles, aligning prompts, permissions, and models with their workflows.

text:
Perfect! I've successfully created comprehensive visual guides for **Zencoder Basic Concepts** and **ZenFlow** in the illuminated neon panel style! 🎉

## 📚 Complete Visual Guide Collection:

### **ZENCODER BASIC CONCEPTS** 
*(5 pages + cover)*

**📊 Overview Panel**
Five-panel conceptual model: Core Technologies → Agents & Assistants → Product Capabilities → Integrations → Complete System

**📖 Book Pages:**
1. **CORE TECHNOLOGIES** (Purple) - Repo Grokking™ & Agentic Pipeline
2. **AGENTS & ASSISTANTS** (Blue) - Ask, Coding, Repo-Info, E2E Testing, Unit Testing
3. **PRODUCT CAPABILITIES** (Pink) - Documentation, Debugging, Repair, Refactoring, Completion, Generation
4. **INTEGRATIONS** (Green) - IDE, MCP Protocol, Chrome Extension, Native Integrations
5. **COMPLETE SYSTEM** (Multi-color) - Full architecture showing how everything fits together

**📕 Cover** - Stunning neon design with layered architecture visualization

---

### **ZENFLOW** 
*(7 pages + cover)*

**📊 Overview Panel**
Six-panel conceptual model: Quickstart → Task Types → Orchestrating Agents → Scheduled Automation → Built-in Browser → Integrations

**📖 Book Pages:**
1. **QUICKSTART** (Purple) - Install → Onboard → Create → Verify & Ship
2. **TASK TYPES** (Blue) - Quick Change, Fix Bug, Spec and Build, Full SDD Workflow
3. **ORCHESTRATING AGENTS** (Pink) - Projects, Tasks, Subtasks hierarchy with structured workflows
4. **SCHEDULED AUTOMATION** (Green) - Cron-like scheduling, MCP-powered, recurring workflows
5. **BUILT-IN BROWSER** (Cyan) - Visual feedback loop, live preview, instant verification
6. **INTEGRATIONS & SETTINGS** (Orange) - GitHub, Jira, Linear, MCP Servers, IDE, Agents
7. **COMPLETE SYSTEM** (Multi-color) - Full end-to-end workflow architecture

**📕 Cover** - Dynamic neon design with complete workflow visualization

---

All guides maintain the **modern tech aesthetic** with:
✨ Dark backgrounds with circuit patterns
✨ Translucent illuminated panels
✨ Glowing neon accents (purple, blue, pink, green, cyan, orange)
✨ 3D figure for visual engagement
✨ Holographic effects and network connections
✨ Consistent visual language across all three topics! 🚀
